title: About
text: About this site

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
This is a hands on exercise 1 exploration.
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)
exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Histogram of Exam Data
First, we compare exam data using a histogram

hist(exam_data$MATHS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
ggplot objects

ggplot(data=exam_data)








element into the plot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS))









geometric objects:
geom_bar

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








geom_dotplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)








geom_histogram

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       








we can modify the geometric object by changing geom()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  








modify by changing aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")








geom_density()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()








plotting 2 kernel density lines by using color

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()








geom_boxplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()            








notched plot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)








geom_violin()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()








geom_point()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()            








combination of geom objects

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
statistic function

stat()
stat_summary()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)               








combine geom() and stat()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)          








how to add best fit curve on a scatterplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)










facets
facet_wrap()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)








facet_grid()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)










coordinates
vertical coordinate

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








using coord_flip()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()








changing the x and y axis range

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))










themes
bar chart using gray color

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()








using theme_classic()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()








using theme_minimal()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: Home
text: This website is my coursework portfolio for ISSS608: Visual Analytics and Applications. It showcases my learning journey through Hands‑on Exercises, In‑class Exercises, and Take‑home Exercises.

title: Home
text: This website is my coursework portfolio for ISSS608: Visual Analytics and Applications. It showcases my learning journey through Hands‑on Exercises, In‑class Exercises, and Take‑home Exercises.

title: Home
text:
2 Motivation
To me, data tells a story, I believe that data becomes meaningful when it is visualized effectively. Through this course, I aim to strengthen my ability to transform raw data into clear, insightful visuals that support better decision‑making.

title: Home
text:
3 Coursework Overview

Hands‑on Exercises: Step‑by‑step practice sessions to apply new concepts done before every week’s lecture
In‑class Exercises: Activities done during weekly lecture to reinforce learning.

Take‑home Exercises:

title: Home
text:
4 Looking Ahead
By the end of this course, I hope to build a strong foundation in visual analytics and apply these skills to real‑world datasets. This portfolio will serve as both a record of my progress and a resource for future projects.

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
This is a hands on exercise 2 exploration.
In this exercise, ggrepel, ggthemes, hrbrthemes and patchwork will be used for exploration.
pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse)
We will import the data now
exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
1 ggrepel
This pacakage is an extension of ggplot2 which provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")








Here’s another example of using ggrepel

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
2 ggthemes
ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes. The themes are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 









2.1 ggtheme package: The Economist theme

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()










2.2 ggtheme package: hrbthems
This package provides a base theme that focuses on typographic elements, including where various labels are placed as well as the fonts that are used.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()








Utilizing the elements of axis_title_size, base_size and grid to modify the above plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
3 Creating Composite Graphics: pathwork methods
While several ggplot2 extensions—such as gridExtra::grid.arrange() and cowplot::plot_grid()—allow you to combine multiple graphs, the patchwork package offers an intuitive approach. Specifically built for the ggplot2 ecosystem, patchwork simplifies the creation of complex layouts using a syntax that feels like writing a simple equation. General syntax of patchwork are:
The Plus Sign (+): Used to place plots side-by-side in a two-column layout.
Parentheses (): Used to group subplots together, allowing you to treat multiple graphs as a single unit within a larger layout.
The Division Sign (/): Used to stack plots vertically in a two-row layout.

3.1 Combining two ggplot2 graphs
First, we create each single graph naming it p1, p2, p3

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")
p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

After creating the graphs, we can utilize th simple patchwork syntax to create a composite of two histograms

p1 + p2










3.2 Combining three ggplot2 graphs
We aren’t just limited to two grids, we can plot more complex composite using the below syntax:

The Slash Sign (/): Places plots on top of one another (vertical stacking)
The Horizontal Slash (|): Places plots side-by-side (horizontal stacking)
The Parentheses (()): Acts just like the normal parentheses, dictating the order of operations so we can nest complex layouts within a single structure.


(p1 / p2) | p3










3.3 Creating a composite figure with tag
Patchwork also provides an auto-tagging function to identify subplots in text.

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')










3.4 Creating figure with insert
Patchwork offers the inset_element() function.We can define the exact boundaries of your inset using a coordinate system from 0 to 1.

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)










3.5 Creating a composite figure by using patchwork and ggtheme
We can combine ggthemes and patchwork. Here’s an example of a patchwork function and theme_economist()

patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
In this third hands-on exercise, we practice programming interactive data visualisation.
ggiraph and plotlyr packages will be used for this exercise.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
1 ggiraph
ggiraph is an htmlwidget and an extension of ggplot2.This package allows to make ggplot interactive. Interactive is made with ggplot geometries that can understand three arguments:

tooltip: a dataset column that provides text to display when the user hovers over chart elements
onclick : a dataset column that specifies a JavaScript function to run when elements are clicked
data_id: a dataset column that assigns unique identifiers to elements, enabling selection and linking

When used inside a Shiny application, elements tagged with data_id acn be dynamically selected and manipulated on both the client an server sides.

1.1 Tooltip effect with tooltip aesthetic

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
2 Importing Data
We will import the data now

exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
3 Interactice Data Visualisation - ggiraph
ggiraph is an htmlwidget and an extension of ggplot2.This package allows to make ggplot interactive. Interactive is made with ggplot geometries that can understand three arguments:

tooltip: a dataset column that provides text to display when the user hovers over chart elements
onclick : a dataset column that specifies a JavaScript function to run when elements are clicked
data_id: a dataset column that assigns unique identifiers to elements, enabling selection and linking

When used inside a Shiny application, elements tagged with data_id acn be dynamically selected and manipulated on both the client an server sides.

3.1 Tooltip effect with tooltip aesthetic
The example below illustrated how to build an interactive statistical plot using the ggiraph package. The workflow has two main steps:

First, create a standard ggplot object.
Then, wrap that plot with girafe() from ggiraph to produce an interactive svg graphic.


p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x= MATHS)) + 
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                      breaks = NULL)

This process involves two steps. First, we use an interactive version of a ggplot2 geometry (for example, geom_dotplot_interactive()) to build the base chart. Second, wrap the plot with girafe() from ggiraph to produce an interactive svg object that can be displayed on an html page.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
1 Check, install and launch packages
First, the codes below will be used to check, install and launch the packages needed

pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, patchwork, DT, tidyverse)

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
4 Interactivity
By hovering the mouse over a specific data point, the corresponding student ID will appear.

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)






4.1 Displaying multiple information on tooltip
The tooltip content can be customized by creating a new field, as shown in the code example below

exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS))
p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks = NULL)

In the first few lines of the code chunk, a new variable called tooltip is created. This variable combines text from the ID and CLASS columns into a single field. Later in the code, this newly defined tooltip field is used as the aesthetic for displaying tooltips in the plot.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
5 Interactivity
By hovering the mouse over a specific data point, the corresponding student ID will appear.

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)






5.1 Customising Tooltip Style
The code chunk below uses opts_tooltip() of ggiraph to customize tooltip rendering by add css declarations.

tooltip_css <- "background-color:white; #<<
font-style:bold; color:black;" #<<

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)

The tooltip is styled with a black background, while the text appears in bold white font.

girafe(
  ggobj = p, 
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(
      css = tooltip_css))
)





Refer to Customizing girafe objects to learn more about how to customise ggiraph objects.


5.2 Displaying statistics on tooltip
The following code chunk demonstrates a more advanced way to customize tooltips. In this case, a function is applied to calculate the 90% confidence interval of the mean. The resulting statistics are then incorporated into the tooptil display.

tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = 0.01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales:: number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE), 
) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS,
                   tooltip = after_stat(
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),
    fun.data = "mean_se",
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "light pink"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, size = 0.2
  )
girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)





This bar chart compares average MATHS scores across four racial groups. The error bars suggest that while there’s some variation within each group, the Chinese group consistently scores higher, and the Malay group tends to score lower.


5.3 Hover effect with data_id aesthetic
The following code chunk demonstrates the second interactive feature available in ggiraph, which is data_id.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = CLASS),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)





Interactivity: When elements are linked to a data_id, such as CLASS, they become highlighted whenever the mouse pointer hovers over them. The default value of the hover css is hover_css = “fill:orange;”
We will try modifying the color into pink.

girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill:pink;")
  )
)   







5.4 Styling hover effect
In the following code chunk, css codes are used to change the highlighting effect

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes( data_id = CLASS),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6, 
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity: 0.2;")
  )
)





Interactivity: Elements associated with a data_id, such as CLASS, will be highlighted upon mouse over.


5.5 Combining tooltip and hover effect
The chunk demonstrates how to combine two interactive features in ggiraph: tooltips and hover effects. In this example, tooltips display additional information when the cursor hovers over a point, while the hover effect highlights elements associated with a data_id.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

Interactivity: When elements are linked to a data_id, such as CLASS, they are highlighted as the mouse hovers over them, while the tooltip simultaneously displays the corresponding CLASS value.

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
  )
)







5.6 Click effect with onclick
onclick argument of ggiraph provides hyperlink interactivity on the web. A code example is provided below:

exam_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
"https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618)





Interactivity: When a data object is linked to a web document, clicking on the element will open that document in the browser.


5.7 Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
The code below creates a multiple views visualisation.

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks = NULL)
p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) + 
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(code = print(p1+p2),
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 3,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
      )
    )





The data_id aesthetic is essential for linking observations across plots, while the tooltip aesthetic is optional but provides useful information when hovering over a point.
When a data point in one dotplot is selected, the corresponding observation in the second visualization is highlighted as well, ENGLISH in this case. To build these coordinates multiple views, the programming strategy applied is to use the appropriate interactive functions gfrom ggiraph to generate the linked views. Then, employ the patchwork function from the patchwork package inside girafe() to arrange and render the interactive coordinated plots.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
6 Interactive Data Visualisation - plotly methods
Plotly’s R library enables the creation of interactive web graphics, either by converting ggplot2 charts or by using its own interface to the MIT‑licensed JavaScript library plotly.js, which is built on the grammar of graphics. Unlike other Plotly platforms, plotly for R is completely free and open source. 
There are two ways to create interactive graph by using plotly which are plot_ly() and ggplotly()

6.1 Creating an interactive scetterplot using plot_ly() method
The tabset below shows an example of a basic interactive plot created by using plot_ly().

The plotThe code chunk










plot_ly(data = exam_data,
        x = ~MATHS,
        y = ~ENGLISH,
        type = "scatter",
        mode = "markers")





6.2 Working with visual variable using plot_ly() method
In the code chunk below, the color argument is mapped to a qualitative variable specifically, RACE.

The plotThe code chunk








Interactive: click on the color symbol at the legend


plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~ENGLISH, 
        y = ~MATHS, 
        color = ~RACE)





6.3 Creating an interactive scatterplot using ggplotly() method
The code chunk belows displays a plot by using ggplotly().

The plotThe code chunk










p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)
In this code, ggplotly() is added as compared to to using the plotly() method





6.4 Coordinated Multiple Views using plotly() method
Building a coordinated linked plot in plotly can be broken down into three main steps:

Use highlight_key() from the plotly package to define the shared data.
Create two scatterplots with ggplot2 functions.
Finally, arrange them side‑by‑side using subplot() from the plotly package.


The plotThe code chunk








Clicking on a point in one scatterplot automatically selects and highlights the corresponding point in the other scatterplot.


d <- highlight_key(exam_data)
p1 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

p2 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
subplot(ggplotly(p1),
        ggplotly(p2))



The function highlight_key() generates an object of class crosstalk::SharedData. For more details on how crosstalk works, you can explore its documentation.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
section: 7 Example of an interactive plot using plotly
text:
7 Example of an interactive plot using plotly
The tabset below shows an example of a basic interactive plot created by using plot_ly().

The plot


plot_ly(data = exam_data, x = ~MATHS, y = ~ENGLISH, type = “scatter”, mode = “markers”) ### The code chunk

plot_ly(data = exam_data,
        x = ~MATHS,
        y = ~ENGLISH,
        type = "scatter",
        mode = "markers")

title: Hands-On Exercise3
section: 7 Interactive data visualization using Crosstalk Methods
text:
7 Interactive data visualization using Crosstalk Methods
Crosstalk is an extension of the htmlwidgets framework. It provides classes, functions, and conventions that enable cross‑widget interactions, such as linked brushing and filtering.

7.1 Interactive data table using DT Package
The DT package is a wrapper around the JavaScript library DataTables. With DT, R data objects can be rendered as interactive HTML tables, typically within R Markdown or Shiny applications.

DT::datatable(exam_data, class= "compact")







7.2 Linked brushing using Crosstalk Method
v Key takeaway from this method:

The highlight() function from the plotly package configures options for brushing (highlighting) across multiple plots. These options are mainly intended for linking multiple Plotly graphs, and behavior may vary when linking Plotly with other htmlwidget packages via crosstalk. In some cases, other widgets (e.g., leaflet) will respect these settings, such as persistent selection.
The bscols() helper from the crosstalk package makes it easy to arrange HTML elements side by side. While it can be called directly from the console, it is especially useful in R Markdown documents. Note that using bscols() will load all of Bootstrap. :::

Now, we move on to the second part of the exercise. Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
3 Interavtice Data Visualisation - ggiraph
ggiraph is an htmlwidget and an extension of ggplot2.This package allows to make ggplot interactive. Interactive is made with ggplot geometries that can understand three arguments:

tooltip: a dataset column that provides text to display when the user hovers over chart elements
onclick : a dataset column that specifies a JavaScript function to run when elements are clicked
data_id: a dataset column that assigns unique identifiers to elements, enabling selection and linking

When used inside a Shiny application, elements tagged with data_id acn be dynamically selected and manipulated on both the client an server sides.

3.1 Tooltip effect with tooltip aesthetic
The example below illustrated how to build an interactive statistical plot using the ggiraph package. The workflow has two main steps:

First, create a standard ggplot object.
Then, wrap that plot with girafe() from ggiraph to produce an interactive svg graphic.


p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x= MATHS)) + 
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                      breaks = NULL)

This process involves two steps. First, we use an interactive version of a ggplot2 geometry (for example, geom_dotplot_interactive()) to build the base chart. Second, wrap the plot with girafe() from ggiraph to produce an interactive svg object that can be displayed on an html page.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
section: 8 Overview of Programming Animated Statistical Graphics
text:
8 Overview of Programming Animated Statistical Graphics
When presenting a visually‑driven data story, animation often captures the audience’s attention more effectively than static graphics and leaves a stronger impression. We will explore how to build animated visualizations using the gganimate and plotly packages. Along the way, we will also practice reshaping data with tidyr and performing data processing, wrangling, and transformations with dplyr.

8.1 Basic Concepts of Animation
An animation isn’t a single plot that moves. Instead, it’s a sequence of individual plots assembled into frames, much like a flip book or traditional cartoon. Each frame represents a subset of the data, and when stitched together, these frames create the illusion of motion. The subsets drive the flow of the animation, making the story unfold dynamically.  ### Terminology Before diving into the steps for building an animated statistical graph, it’s useful to clarify some key concepts:

Frame: In an animated line chart, each frame corresponds to a specific time point or category. As the animation progresses from one frame to the next, the data points update to reflect the new subset of information.
Animation Attributes: These are the settings that define how the animation behaves. Examples include the duration of each frame, the easing function that controls the smoothness of transitions, and whether playback begins from the current frame or restarts from the beginning.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
text:
9 Getting started

9.1 Check, install and launch packages
Below are brief definition of each package that we will be using in the exercise: - plotly: A library for building interactive statistical graphics.

gganimate: An extension of ggplot2 that enables the creation of animated statistical plots.
gifski: Converts video frames into GIF animations, using pngquant for efficient color palettes and temporal dithering. It produces high‑quality animated GIFs with thousands of colors per frame.
gapminder: A sample dataset derived from Gapminder.org, often used for visualization exercises. In this context, it provides a convenient country_colors scheme.
tidyverse: A collection of modern R packages designed for data science workflows, including data wrangling, transformation, and communication tasks. It also supports the creation of static statistical graphics.


pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)



9.2 Importing Data
In this part, we’ll be working with the Data worksheet from the GlobalPopulation Excel workbook. The code chunk below will read the Excel workbook

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("../data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%
  mutate_at(col, as.factor) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

Another version that we can code with the same output is using across instead of mutate_at

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("../data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%
  mutate(across(col, as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

title: Hands-On Exercise3
section: 10 Animated Data Visualization using gganimate method
text:
10 Animated Data Visualization using gganimate method
gganimate extends the grammar of graphics in ggplot2 to incorporate animation. It does this by introducing new grammar components that can be added to a plot object, allowing you to control how the visualization evolves over time.

transition_*(): Specifies how the data should be distributed and connected across time.
view_*(): Defines how the positional scales (axes, ranges) adjust throughout the animation.
shadow_*(): Determines how data from other time points is displayed at the current moment.
enter_() / exit_(): Control how new data appears and how old data fades out during the animation.
ease_aes(): Sets the easing behavior for aesthetic transitions, influencing how smoothly visual properties change.


10.1 Building a static Population Bubble Plot

ggplot(globalPop, aes(x=Old, y=Young,
                      size=Population,
                      color=Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.7,
             show.legend=FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2,12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}',
       x = '%Aged',
       y = '%Young')










10.2 Building an animated Population Bubble Plot
In the following code,

transition_time() from gganimate is used to animate the plot across distinct time states, such as different years.
ease_aes() controls how visual properties (aesthetics) change during transitions. By default, easing is linear, but we can choose alternative methods such as quadratic, cubic, quartic, quintic, sine, circular, exponential, elastic, back, or bounce to adjust the smoothness and style of the animation.


ggplot(globalPop, aes(x=Old, y=Young,
                      size=Population,
                      color=Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.7,
             show.legend=FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2,12)) +
  labs(title = 'Yea: {frame_time}',
       x = '%Aged',
       y = '%Young') +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes('linear')










10.3 Animated Data Visualizuation using plotly method
In the Plotly R package, both ggplotly() and plot_ly() support key frame animations through the frame argument or aesthetic. This allows plots to evolve across distinct time states or categories. They also provide an ids argument/aesthetic, which ensures that objects with the same identifier transition smoothly between frames. This helps maintain object constancy, so elements appear to persist rather than being redrawn from scratch.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
section: 11 Building an animated Population Bubble Plot
text:
11 Building an animated Population Bubble Plot

The plotThe code chunk










bbp <- ggplot(globalPop, 
              aes(x=Old,
                  y=Young,
                  size=Population,
                  color=Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size=Population,
                 frame=Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2,12)) +
  labs(x = '%Aged',
       y = '%Young') +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')

ggplotly(bbp)     
Normally, ggplot2 adds a legend when you map aesthetics like color, size, or shape to variables. Setting legend.position = “none” tells ggplot2 not to display the legend, even if one would normally be create where this is useful when the legend is redundant.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
section: 11 Building an animated Population Bubble Plot using ggplotly()
text:
11 Building an animated Population Bubble Plot using ggplotly()

The plotThe code chunk










bbp <- ggplot(globalPop, 
              aes(x=Old,
                  y=Young,
                  size=Population,
                  color=Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size=Population,
                 frame=Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2,12)) +
  labs(x = '%Aged',
       y = '%Young') +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')

ggplotly(bbp)     
Normally, ggplot2 adds a legend when you map aesthetics like color, size, or shape to variables. Setting legend.position = "none" tells ggplot2 not to display the legend, even if one would normally be create where this is useful when the legend is redundant.

title: Hands-On Exercise3
section: 12 Building an animated Population Bubble Plot using plotly()
text:
12 Building an animated Population Bubble Plot using plotly()

The plotThe code chunk










bbp2 <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly( x = ~Old,
           y = ~Young,
           size = ~Population,
           color = ~Continent,
           sizes = c(2,100),
           frame = ~Year,
           text = ~Country,
           hoverinfo = "text",
           type = 'scatter',
           mode = 'markers'
           ) %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)
bbp2
